channel.title: Nature Physics
channel.description: Nature Physics offers news and reviews alongside top-quality research papers in a monthly publication, covering the entire spectrum of physics. Physics addresses the properties and interactions of matter and energy, and plays a key role in the development of a broad range of technologies. To reflect this, Nature Physics covers all areas of pure and applied physics research. The journal focuses on core physics disciplines, but is also open to a broad range of topics whose central theme falls within the bounds of physics.
channel.dc:rights: © 2026 Macmillan Publishers Limited, part of Springer Nature. All rights reserved.
channel.prism:copyright: © 2026 Macmillan Publishers Limited, part of Springer Nature. All rights reserved.

item.title: Two-dimensional non-equilibrium melting of charged colloids
item.content:encoded:
Nature Physics, Published online: 02 January 2026; doi:10.1038/s41567-025-03121-2

Non-equilibrium two-dimensional melting is less understood than its equilibrium counterpart. Now it is shown that topologically driven melting in a two-dimensional crystal of charged colloids is the same irrespective of the mechanisms that generate the defects
item.dc:title: Two-dimensional non-equilibrium melting of charged colloids
item.dc:creator: Ankit D. Vyas
Philipp W. A. Schönhöfer
Terrence M. Hopkins
Andrew D. Hollingsworth
Stefano Sacanna
Sharon C. Glotzer
Paul Chaikin

item.title: Challenges and opportunities in orbitronics
item.content:encoded:
Nature Physics, Published online: 31 December 2025; doi:10.1038/s41567-025-03143-w

Orbitronics uses the electron’s orbital angular momentum to enable next-generation memory and computing technologies. This Perspective outlines recent advances, key challenges and future directions in this rapidly evolving field.
item.dc:title: Challenges and opportunities in orbitronics
item.dc:creator: Shunsuke Fukami
Kyung-Jin Lee
Mathias Kläui

item.title: Experimental evidence of a spatially textured electron fluid
item.content:encoded:
Nature Physics, Published online: 30 December 2025; doi:10.1038/s41567-025-03133-y

In most metals, free electrons form a homogeneous and isotropic fluid. However, a periodically modulated electronic fluid — known as a liquid charge density wave — is thought to form when electrons interact strongly with the vibrations of the crystalline host. This state is now observed using ultrafast electron diffraction.
item.dc:title: Experimental evidence of a spatially textured electron fluid

item.title: Observation of a hidden charge density wave liquid
item.content:encoded:
Nature Physics, Published online: 30 December 2025; doi:10.1038/s41567-025-03108-z

Liquid charge density wave order is thought to occur in many correlated electron systems but has not been observed experimentally. Now, a liquid-like electronic state is shown to emerge in a transition metal dichalcogenide on photoexcitation.
item.dc:title: Observation of a hidden charge density wave liquid
item.dc:creator: Joshua S. H. Lee
Thomas M. Sutter
Goran Karapetrov
Pietro Musumeci
Anshul Kogar

item.title: Interplay of orbital angular momentum and chirality
item.content:encoded:
Nature Physics, Published online: 29 December 2025; doi:10.1038/s41567-025-03113-2

Orbital angular momentum textures can drive orbital currents and enable applications in orbitronics. This Perspective shows how polarization-dependent angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy can visualize orbital textures in momentum space.
item.dc:title: Interplay of orbital angular momentum and chirality
item.dc:creator: Dongjin Oh
Hendrik Bentmann
Riccardo Comin
